# Consideration in Contracts

Consideration is something of value that each party gives up to make a contract
binding. In common-law systems a promise is generally not enforceable unless it is
supported by consideration: each side must offer something, whether money, goods, a
service, or a promise to act or refrain from acting. It is what distinguishes a
bargain from a mere gift.

## What counts as valid

Consideration must be sufficient but need not be adequate, meaning courts check that
something of value was exchanged but do not weigh whether the deal was fair. A
promise to do what one is already legally bound to do usually does not count. Past
acts performed before a promise was made are generally not valid consideration,
because they were not given in exchange for the promise.
